title: To oppose the permitting of deep seabed mining and exploration for deep seabed mining, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This bill wants the President to tell other countries to stop giving permission for deep seabed mining. Deep seabed mining is when companies dig up materials from the bottom of the ocean. The bill says we don't know enough about how this mining could hurt ocean animals and plants. It also says we need to do more scientific research and create better rules to protect the ocean before allowing this type of mining to happen.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR E86-87)

sections.title: Section 1 Opposition to Permitting of Deep Seabed Mining and Exploration for Deep Seabed Mining
sections.text: Congress has found several important facts about deep seabed mining. The United Nations has determined that the world's oceans are in great danger from climate change, resource extraction, and pollution. The United Nations created a goal in two thousand fifteen called Sustainable Development Goal fourteen to protect and sustainably use oceans, seas, and marine resources. This goal commits countries to manage marine ecosystems in ways that avoid major harmful impacts and make them stronger. Scientists and policy experts around the world agree that deep seabed mining presents major risks to marine environments. These risks include the direct loss of unique and ecologically important species that live only in these areas. Mining would create large clouds of sediment that would harm ecosystems far beyond the actual mining locations. The noise pollution from mining would cause physical and behavioral stress to marine animals. Mining would likely contaminate commercially important food fish. It would also likely harm the ocean's ability to store carbon, which helps fight climate change. The United Nations Convention on Biological Diversity encourages member countries to make sure that before deep seabed mineral mining is allowed, the impacts on marine environments and biodiversity are thoroughly researched. Countries should understand the risks to marine ecosystems well enough and impose sufficient rules and conditions to ensure that mining does not cause harmful effects to marine environments and biodiversity. A two thousand twenty-two United Nations Environment Programme report states that financing deep seabed mining activities does not align with Sustainable Blue Economy Finance Principles. There is currently not enough scientific information about the deep sea and related marine ecosystems to fully and accurately assess all the risks and impacts of deep seabed mining activities. Congress believes that no deep seabed mining should happen in international seabed areas unless and until the International Seabed Authority adopts a complete and binding set of rules according to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, particularly Article one hundred forty-five of that Convention. There is currently not enough scientific understanding of the risks and impacts of deep seabed mining on ocean and related ecosystems, and no consensus among experts. The International Seabed Authority should only adopt rules based on a comprehensive scientific understanding of and consensus on the potential risks and impacts of deep seabed mining on ocean ecosystems and communities and activities that depend on ocean ecosystems, including fisheries, indigenous peoples, and coastal communities. Such comprehensive scientific understanding and consensus should only be developed based on sufficient scientific baseline information about the full impacts of deep seabed mining on marine and related ecosystems. No deep seabed mining should be permitted in international seabed areas unless it is conducted in a way and under clear and enforceable rules that effectively protect the marine environment from harmful effects, do not pose a risk to communities that depend on ocean ecosystems, and prevent damage to marine plants and animals. These protections must be consistent with obligations in Article one hundred forty-five of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and relevant global commitments for conservation and sustainable use of marine environments. The President must direct the United States representative to each relevant international organization where the United States is a member or observer to use the voice, vote, and influence of the United States in such organizations to call for a moratorium (temporary stop) on permitting deep seabed mining and exploration for deep seabed mining, and to oppose investments in or other financing or support of deep seabed mining and exploration for deep seabed mining. The President must continue these actions until the President submits to the appropriate congressional committees a certification and a report as described in the following parts of this section. The certification must state that deep seabed mining regulations have been created by the International Seabed Authority, and that such regulations are informed by scientific consensus on impacts to ocean ecosystems and will ensure effective protection of the marine environment from harmful effects of deep seabed mining. The report must include the details of the deep seabed mining regulations created by the International Seabed Authority, the scientific consensus on the risks and impacts of such regulations on ocean ecosystems and communities that depend on ocean ecosystems, and the methods by which such regulations ensure effective protection of the marine environment from harmful effects of deep seabed mining.
